title: Fröhlich-type Polarons in Isotopically Enriched Hexagonal Boron Nitride
authors: Ioannis Chatzakis, Timur Abdilov, Elliot Walker, Jaime Freitas, Song Liu, James H. Edgar
abstract: Exciton-phonon interactions play a central role in defining the optical response of hexagonal boron nitride (hBN), yet their quantitative determination has remained incomplete. Here, we reveal the Fröhlich-type exciton-phonon coupling in boron-10-enriched hBN using low-temperature cathodoluminescence. We resolve the indirect exciton 5.95$\pm$0.02 eV together with its longitudinal optical (LO) phonon replica detuned by 184$\pm$56 meV, enabling the extraction of a Fröhlich coupling constant $α$=0.159 and a larger exciton binding energy of 161 meV, larger than previously reported values for natural-abundance hBN, which is attributed to isotope enrichment. The inferred polaron radius exceeds the lattice constant, indicating large-polaron behavior. We deduced an exciton scattering time ~of 97 fs, corresponding to a homogeneous linewidth of ~6.76 meV. We further obtain a polaron binding energy of ~48 meV and an effective mass of 1.045 $m_0$. These results provide a direct quantitative characterization of exciton-phonon coupling in isotopically engineered hBN and establish a foundation for tailoring its phonon-polaritonic and quantum-optical properties.
summary: This paper demonstrates that enriching hBN with the lighter boron-10 isotope modifies the lattice dynamics and dielectric screening, leading to enhanced exciton binding energy and the formation of large Fröhlich-type polarons. By using cathodoluminescence, the researchers provide a quantitative measurement of exciton-phonon coupling strengths. This work establishes a foundation for engineering the optical and quantum properties of hBN through isotopic control.
main_problem: The quantitative determination of exciton-phonon interactions and the role of isotopic composition in defining the optical response of hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) remains incomplete.
main_result: The study reveals Fröhlich-type exciton-phonon coupling in boron-10-enriched hBN, demonstrating that isotopic engineering can increase exciton binding energy and enhance polaron formation.
method: Low-temperature (6 K) cathodoluminescence (CL) spectroscopy was used to resolve exciton and phonon-replica peaks, complemented by Raman spectroscopy and AFM for structural characterization.
model_or_system: The physical system is isotopically enriched (99.22 at% 10B) and natural-abundance hexagonal boron nitride (hBN), modeled using the Fröhlich interaction and Feynman's path-integral theory for polarons.
key_observables: Indirect exciton energy (5.95 eV), LO phonon replica detuning (184 meV), Fröhlich coupling constant (alpha), exciton binding energy, and phonon energy shifts.
important_parameters: Fröhlich coupling constant alpha = 0.159, exciton binding energy of 161 meV, polaron binding energy of ~48 meV, and exciton scattering time of ~97 fs.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the validity of the Fröhlich interaction model for long-range coupling and uses the Kanaya et al. diffusion model for electron penetration depth.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows Raman spectra comparing natural and 10B-enriched hBN; Figure 3 shows AFM thickness profiles; Figure 4 shows CL spectra with defect series; Figure 5 shows high-energy CL features; Figure 6 illustrates the phonon-mediated transition mechanism.
paper_structure: The paper begins with material synthesis and structural verification (Raman/AFM), proceeds to the identification of excitonic and defect-related spectral features via CL, and concludes with a quantitative analysis of exciton-phonon coupling and isotopic effects.
why_interesting: The study provides a direct quantitative characterization of how lattice engineering (isotopic substitution) can be used to tailor phonon-polaritonic and quantum-optical properties in 2D materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Raman spectrum for hexagonal boron nitride at 295 K. The green (natural) and blue (10B isotope enriched) lines are the experimental data, and the dashed lines are the fits with Lorentzian function. The peak positions are at 1365 𝑐𝑚−1 and 1393 𝑐𝑚−1 respectively.

Fig.2 (Left) Spatial Raman intensity map of the 𝐸2𝑔 mode of the 10B-enriched hBN crystal. The uniform contrast across the scanned region indicates high structural and isotopic homogeneity. (Right) Corresponding histogram of the Raman peak position extracted from the map, showing a narrow distribution centered at the characteristic phonon frequency. The small spread confirms the high spatial uniformity of the sample, which is essential for reliable quantitative analysis of exciton–phonon coupling.
Fig. 3 AFM image of the sample (top) and corresponding height profile (bottom) showing a thickness of ~4μm, confirming its three- dimensional character.

Fig. 4 CL spectrum at 6 K. The large amplitude of the D series of lines in the ~ 5.2 to 5.77 eV range, compared to the peak amplitude above 5.8 eV, indicates the existence of structural defects. The positions of each emission band (in eV) are annotated with the corresponding colors.

Fig. 5 Expanded view of the high-energy region of Fig. 4. The green area highlights the transition located at ~5.56 eV. The brown color peak at ~ 5.95 eV corresponds to the indirect exciton, while its LO phonon mode replica, which appears at ~ 5.77 eV, is shown in blue. The transition at 6.02 eV is represented by the green-gray Lorentzian.
